So a huge number of businesses engage unwittingly, unintentionally in customer sharing. It's orthodontist doesn't. Once he's working on your kid, that's it. Right. But restaurant. Absolutely. Clothing store. Right. So you, like me, you may have one, you patronize more than others, but this does not prevent you from four or five times a year buying something somewhere else because it cut your eye or you were at the mall and were bored or whatever. You go to more than one restaurant for diversity, but often just because you're going out with friends on Friday night and nobody has a profound preference, et cetera. Would you be more likely to share less and patronize somebody more if after the first time you went to the restaurant, you got the big welcome to the family box, right? With gift cards to give somebody 50 cool recipes from our celebrity chef book, a spatula thing to get toast out of a toaster and not burn your fingers and on and on and on. That's sticky. Absolutely. It reminds you to go and to tell people about this cool place who has treated you in such a cool and unusual way. And it shows that at least to me, it would show that they actually care. So now, if you replicate that experience eight, 10, 12, 14 times a year, less, but still, it's happening again and again and again. It's unexpected. It's abnormal. It's sometimes useful, sometimes just cool. Does it increase your frequency of patronage and your likelihood of referral? Yeah. You bet. Yeah, you bet. You bet. So who should you gift? Should you gift everybody? Should everyone get the same gifts? You can. At bare minimum. Everybody should get some. You may wish to divide customers, a customer, B customer, C customer, and that is by value of their own spending, by their referral activity, not said to them, but you may want to invest 25 bucks in the gift for the customer and $5 in the gift for the C customer. The C customers may get it by mail, et cetera. So you may spend differentially based on their value to you, but you ought to spend. Well, let's talk about that. How much should you spend? So what's it worth you to keep on? Oh, okay. That's a little bit different. So first it was how much is it worth it for you to attract them in the first place? Now it's how much is it worth it for you to keep on? So customer replacement is a very expensive thing because you need two for one. You've got to replace the one you lost and you still need to gain one. So what's it worth you to keep somebody? In a lot of businesses it's like one month more, one season more. So you just went in and bought clothes for summer. If I can get you to come back in the fall rather than not get you to come back for what's that worth? It's a predictable dollar value. If you're running a restaurant of say, Fleming's or Morton's caliber, most people don't go by themselves. At least two people go. Usually it's a foursome. Tickets $250, $300, $350. If you go four times a year and I could get you to come six times a year, what's that worth? Well, it's worth $500. So now how much of that $500 am I willing to spend six times a year dropping a little gift on you, which is almost certainly going to get you to come one or two more times. Absolutely. No tie, no promote, no you're coming because I'm showing up in a nice and interesting and cool way. So there's math to this, right? They are either going to eat it or they are going to give it to somebody who is going to eat it. So it's not going to get shoved in a drawer and forgotten. It's not going to get thrown in a trash. I got, so I'll tiff with a, I guess I shouldn't mention it, but a business magazine a couple of years ago, when a book came out, we sent promotion kits to the editors of, I don't know, 12 business magazines. And nice, not really expensive, I bought them at OfficeMax. But the aluminum briefcase thing, you know, and a book in there, and I don't know, a mouse pad, you know, we filled the briefcase with stuff. And I got it back from the One Magazine with a really huffy letter that you should know better than to send gifts into a business-to-business environment. Car editors are not permitted to accept, you know. So I sent a big sympathy card. I said, I hope there's no prohibition at your company against getting a sympathy card. Here's my sympathies for working at such a shitty company. So you're probably not going to see my book reviewed in any of those. But for the most part, if there is a rule, it's usually like 50, it's a limit. You can't accept a vendor gift more than 50 bucks, right? So in real highfalutin B2B, IBM, you got to think about this, right? Not that some people don't disobey the rules, right? But in B2B, it's really useful if you do gifts that get shared. So what's hitting me is that, yeah, we're talking about referrals, but this serves a mall couple of purposes. Oh, yeah. Well, retention. Right. In his business, there's month-to-month continuity. Right. There's year-to-year renewal. There's upgrade. In many businesses, the equivalent would be, again, repeat patronage. Will it cause somebody to share their purchases in a category less and be more loyal? Wrong word. Right. Yeah, yeah. Will it cause them to stick with you longer than they would otherwise? Sure. And referrals as well. So is this pastor now more or less likely to talk about Nelson to a peer? Another pastor. If he is getting cool gifts and cool stuff every so often during the course of the year, then if he isn't, absolutely. If he is asked about Nelson by a peer, is he more or less likely to respond enthusiastically if he's being gifted during the course of the year? One of the things that you mentioned just in passing, I want to bring it back up, is not to do it necessarily on a—so it's not perceived as being on a regular basis. It is far better to do it that way than not to do it at all. But when you really mechanize and standardize and automate these things, you sacrifice the surprise package. So it's still fun to open a box, but I know I'm getting a box every month. If I've been with you for more than a year, I kind of know, okay, three times a year it's going to be a coffee cup and a jackknife, and three times a year it's going to be cookies. So you sacrifice some of the while to it when it becomes so predictable. So if you make it appear to be randomized when it is not randomized at all— That's the key, though. It's not randomized at all. It just appears to be. If it's randomized, it doesn't happen. We're going to do this three times a year. Well, what are we going to do? What are we going to do? I don't know, but we'll get around to it while you get around to it. But when it appears to be more randomized and less predictable, then it has greater impact. Fantastic. Again, if you've got a question about this or anything about referral marketing, make sure that you enter it in there. So Dan, we do have some questions here from folks. You're going to love this one. This comes from Stas Ray. What if my business is online only? Which welcome gift could I give them? Well, do you want the smart answer or do you want the answer he would like? Why don't you give him the answer that he would like and then tell him the smart answers? Just do both. All right. So you are now limited, if you're going to do online delivery, you're limited to information and entertainment. Those are the two things that you can do. So you can deliver more of the same. Some version of what you are already doing that is a gift out of the blue. You can deliver more entertainment that is a gift out of the blue. But candidly, there's so much less pizzazz to an online gift than an offline gift. Even kids, it's more and more this way, but even kids are not real thrilled under the Christmas tree with a digital download and that's it. They actually kind of want to open a package. They kind of built into it. So yeah, and look, two generations from now it will probably have been bred out of us because more and more is an email greeting card and a digital download. You don't expect to, you're not trained as you say as a little kid, so now you're not going to open a package with three record albums in it and be all excited and now that becomes a part of your DNA as an adult and you're expected. You won't expect that anymore. You got a code number to use at iTunes. But that's another 10 or 20 years from now. So right now the people you're selling to still grew up opening packages. So you really handicapped yourself if you do digital only. And that kind of falls into the gift category too and not the wow category. I don't know how you... So however, there's no law that says that because you have an e-commerce business or you are in the digital delivery business that prevents you from sending an offline gift unless you are not getting physical addresses, which is dumb and dangerous in and of itself. They need to get the essential and marketing truth. Honestly, if you don't have that and you're essentially in a marketing truth, we'll teach you how to do that properly. But I mean you can be digitally delivering and still offline gift and if that's all you did offline, it would be worth doing. Well just think about the impact of that. I mean think about the impact of that because they're totally not expecting that. So if you kind of go back to our list, so a digitally delivered gift. Do they have permanence in a physical environment? No, I don't think so. Does it actually have a wow... No. Does it have much of a pass along effect? Hard. So again, if you look at Disney, so here are people who understand digital. They get it. They can get the best and the brightest to merrily come and beg to work for them at least for a while and they own digital media up the yin and yang. They just bought in order to expand their YouTube channel. I forget what they bought but they paid $200 million. Now they got 622 digital YouTube channels instead of 22. So they're there. They publish 5 print magazines. This thing, there's enough... D23, you could spend your life online looking at everything they give you. They're sending that box. They're sending that magazine. They're sending me a watch. They're sending me a pen. They're sending me stuff. They're not sending me a picture of a pen by email. I'm not going to put that on my pen holder that sits on my desk that I see every day. I'm not going to display it so you see it. So they get this. So it's not like they're a model you look at and you think like you might judge me. Well, here's an old fart who's anti-internet and doesn't know what the hell he's doing. So you can't say that about Disney. No, right. You just can't. I think anybody watching this, I mean logically would say, well, yeah, even the online guys, okay, I get it. I get it. So yeah, just... Now a bigger, broader answer to him is you ought not have an online-only business in the first place. All right. That's the essential internet marketing truth, though. So let's go to Bryson. Bryson White says, I definitely see the value in this. If you are in a... What if you are in an industry in which there is virtually no repetition and there are no repeat customers? If there are referrals, then that answer is the question. Right. So again, if you're not just about to repeat customers, this just happened to come out of this. This is really all about referrals. If there are referral potential, if there is referral potential, preferably direct, meaning your client can tell somebody else about you who is an eligible client. In some cases, if the transaction value is... I've got to try all the guests. Of course. Be sure to try that one. And while you're at it, do it for both of us. So if the transaction value is high enough, even if there's no direct referral potential, but there's indirect referral potential, meaning reputation, what's going to happen if Joe, the Nuclear Power Plant Manager, asks you, a different Nuclear Power Plant Manager at the trade show, what do you know about XYZ? You can be, matter of fact, we use them. We get bolts from them. Or you can be enthusiastic, we get bolts from them and they're great guys. They're so cool. And so that's an indirect... If any of that potential exists, then the value of this is substantial. If there's almost no repeat. So if you think about a real estate agent, average person buys yourself a house every seven years. So there's a long gap between transactions with the same customer, but there's high referral potential. So it's evident, orthodontist, other than the next kid, if they got more than one kid in the family, I mean, once he envisages lines, he's done. But the referral potential is huge because kids, their four parents flock together, go to little league games together. The Nuclear Power Plant example is B to B. If... Then the other question you have to ask yourself is if there's no repeat transaction, there's no client or customer value beyond the initial transaction. Should I reconfigure the business so that there is? Right. So broad or clear. Now again, that's a big entrepreneurial question. But... And look, there are businesses where the transaction value is so high, the profit made on the sale is so high, that messing around with any of that really doesn't make any sense, especially if there's an inexhaustible supply of them. Let's just go get another one. But in many cases, it's accepting a business the way it is that really could be added onto a business that's changed, modified in a way that would give you repeat transaction, would give you next transaction. Okay, they'll do this with me. What else will they do with me? So if you watch Disney over the past 15 years, they have greatly added to the customer value by adding onto what I call their... Okay, they bought this. Well, they buy this list, the next list. That's why they got in a cruise line business. If they'll come to the parks, so the first next question was, okay, if they'll come to the park, will they stay in a hotel we own and pay twice what they pay to stay in Hilton down the road? Well, yes, they will. If I will get a hotel business. Would they prepay for 50 years of hotels? Yeah? Okay, we'll get in a timeshare business. Gee, they'll come to a park, they'll stay on a hotel, would they get on a boat? Yep, we're in a cruise line business, right? Gee, they ask all the time how we do things, would they pay to go to classes? Yep, they'll pay to go to classes. Crap, a whole lot of them are business people. And they ask questions about how we do business. Would they pay to boom, we're in that business. So they have magnified the customer value enormously, which allows them to spend more money to get one and to spend more money to maintain one because every customer is worth more and every referral is worth more. So there's the question begs questions. Right, yeah, absolutely, and a great answer. So in this session we talked about, and you learned about the different while packages that you can send. We've given you plenty of examples, plenty of things for you to think about from the simple to the more complex. We've also talked about the importance of gifting. We talked about why food is good, but you just want to use exclusively use food. And in our next session, Dan and I will discuss three key elements you need as part of your No BS referral machine.
